title: When It's Time to Talk About Driving
summary: Driving is often tied closely to independence, which makes this one of the harder conversations in caregiving — but a few specific signs make the timing clearer.
author: HarborCare Circle Editorial
seoTitle: When It's Time to Talk About Driving | HarborCare Circle
metaDescription: Driving is often tied closely to independence, which makes this one of the harder conversations in caregiving — but a few specific signs make the timing cl
whatYouNeedToKnow: New dents, scrapes, or getting lost on familiar routes are stronger signals than age alone.
The conversation usually goes better as a series of check-ins than one big talk.
A doctor's input can carry more weight than a family member's alone.

paragraphs: Age by itself isn't a reliable measure of driving safety — what matters more is specific evidence: new dents or scrapes, near-misses, getting lost on routes driven for decades, or family members feeling anxious as a passenger. Any of these are worth taking seriously.

This conversation tends to go better spread across a few smaller check-ins than delivered as one big, high-stakes talk. It also often helps to bring in a doctor, since a clinical recommendation can land differently than the same concern coming from an adult child.

nextSteps: Note specific incidents rather than relying on a general feeling.
Ask their doctor to raise driving safety at the next visit.
Start researching alternative transportation before the conversation, so you have concrete options ready.
disclaimer: General guidance — a formal driving evaluation, sometimes available through occupational therapists, can offer an objective assessment if the family disagrees on timing.

sources.name: Clinician's Guide to Assessing and Counseling Older Drivers
